name: Traditional Catholic Movement
description: The Traditional Catholic Movement encompasses various groups and individuals who believe that the Catholic Church, particularly since the Second Vatican Council (1962-1965), has deviated from traditional doctrines and practices. Proponents generally advocate for the Tridentine Mass (the Mass celebrated before the liturgical reforms following Vatican II), a stricter interpretation of Catholic dogma, and a rejection of what they perceive as modernism and liberalism within the Church. The movement's origins lie in the immediate aftermath of Vatican II, with concerns arising over changes to the liturgy, ecumenism, and the Church's relationship with the modern world. Key evidence cited includes papal documents prior to Vatican II that condemn modernism (e.g., Pascendi Dominici Gregis), claims of doctrinal inconsistencies between pre- and post-Vatican II teachings, and perceived moral decline in society following the Council. Mainstream Catholic scholars and the Vatican generally view Vatican II as a legitimate ecumenical council that clarified and developed Catholic doctrine while remaining consistent with tradition. They argue that the liturgical reforms were intended to make the Mass more accessible and understandable to the faithful, and that ecumenism is a necessary step towards Christian unity. Criticisms of the Traditional Catholic Movement often center on accusations of schism, disobedience to the Pope, and a selective reading of history that ignores the historical development of Catholic doctrine. Some extreme factions within the movement hold sedevacantist views, believing the papacy to be vacant since the death of Pius XII or John XXIII.
subtitle: Restoring the 'True' Catholic Faith

claims.claim: The Second Vatican Council introduced heresy and damaged the Catholic faith.
claims.evidence: Alleged contradictions between pre- and post-Vatican II documents, perceived decline in religious observance after the Council.
claims.criticism: Vatican II clarified and developed doctrine, not contradicting it. The perceived decline is attributed to broader societal changes.

claims.claim: The Tridentine Mass is the only valid form of the Mass.
claims.evidence: Historical precedence, perceived reverence and spiritual efficacy of the Tridentine Mass.
claims.criticism: The Novus Ordo Mass (post-Vatican II) is valid and legitimate, intended to be more accessible to modern congregations.

claims.claim: Modernism and liberalism have infiltrated the Catholic Church.
claims.evidence: Ecumenical dialogues with other religions, perceived tolerance of moral relativism.
claims.criticism: Ecumenism is aimed at Christian unity, and the Church maintains its moral teachings while engaging with the modern world.

keyFigures.name: Archbishop Marcel Lefebvre
keyFigures.role: Founder of the Society of St. Pius X (SSPX)

keyFigures.name: Pope Paul VI
keyFigures.role: Oversaw the implementation of Vatican II, a figure of contention for traditionalists

mythologyConnections: Rejection of modern interpretations of scripture in favor of traditional interpretations
Emphasis on ritual and symbolism considered sacred and unchanging

timeline.event: Promulgation of the Novus Ordo Missae (New Order of the Mass)
Archbishop Lefebvre consecrates bishops without papal approval, leading to schism
Pope Benedict XVI lifts the excommunications of the four SSPX bishops consecrated in 1988

sources.author: Ralph McInerny
sources.work: What Went Wrong with Vatican II
sources.title: Ralph McInerny
